name: Route of the Mills
name_original: Iglesia de Santa María a Quintana del Pidio — circular desde Gumiel de Izán
description: Hike a moderate 10.3-mile (16.6 km) circular route from Gumiel de Izán, exploring vineyards, pine forests, and historic towns in Ribera del
region_intro: Hiking around Gumiel De Izán offers diverse natural landscapes within the province of Burgos, Castile and León. The region is characterized by the verdant Gromejón River valley, extensive cereal fields, and lomas (small hills) covered with vineyards. Hikers can explore areas featuring pine and holm oak forests, alongside the agricultural plains. Best hiking trails around Gumiel De Izán The most popular hiking route is Route of the Mills, a 10.3 miles (16.6 km) trail that takes 4 hours 18 minutes to complete. This route follows the Gromejón River, passing by old flour mills and Roman bridges. Another top favourite among local hikers is Monte de Bahabón Trail, a moderate 7.8 miles (12.6 km) path. This trail leads through pine and holm oak forests, offering views of the surrounding lomas. Local hikers also love the Humedales del Carrascal — circular from Villalba de Duero, a 6.6 miles (10.6 km) trail leading through a verdant river valley and wetlands, often completed in about 2 hours 41 minutes. Hiking around Gumiel De Izán is defined by the Gromejón River valley, picturesque vineyards, and varied forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to moderate trails. The routes in Gumiel De Izán are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 60 hikers have used komoot to explore Gumiel De Izán's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking around Gumiel De Izán?
A: Hiking around Gumiel De Izán offers a diverse range of landscapes. You'll find the verdant Gromejón River valley with its riverside ecosystems, extensive cereal fields, and picturesque vineyards characteristic of the Ribera del Duero wine region. The area also features lomas (small hills) and forests of pine and holm oak, providing varied scenic views throughout the seasons.

Q: Are there any easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or casual walkers?
A: Yes, Gumiel De Izán offers several easy trails. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1037989241/duero-path" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Duero Path&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 3.4-mile (3.4 km) route that provides a pleasant walk. In total, there are 7 easy routes available, making it accessible for various fitness levels.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes in the Gumiel De Izán area?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes available. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1037913714/humedales-del-carrascal-circular-from-villalba-de-duero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Humedales del Carrascal — circular from Villalba de Duero&lt;/a&gt;, which is 6.6 miles (10.6 km) long and takes you through a verdant river valley and wetlands. Another circular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1293787405/sendero-de-la-ribera-del-esgueva-prc-bu-76-circular-desde-cabanes-de-esgueva" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sendero de la Ribera del Esgueva (PRC-BU 76)&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What historical or cultural points of interest can I discover on the trails?
A: Many trails in the region integrate historical and cultural elements. The famous &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1040919201/route-of-the-mills" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route of the Mills&lt;/a&gt; follows the Gromejón River, showcasing old flour mills and ancient Roman bridges like the San Pedro bridge. The town of Gumiel de Izán itself is a Historic-Artistic Site with medieval walls and the impressive Gothic Church of Santa María.

Q: Are there any specific trails that go through vineyards?
A: Yes, the landscape around Gumiel de Izán is part of the Ribera del Duero wine region, and many trails traverse picturesque vineyards. Hiking through these areas offers stunning scenery and a connection to the region's winemaking tradition, with opportunities to see wineries like Bodegas Portia.

Q: What are the best times of year to go hiking in Gumiel De Izán?
A: The diverse landscapes of Gumiel De Izán offer different experiences throughout the seasons. Spring and autumn generally provide pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery, with blooming wildflowers or rich autumnal colors in the vineyards and forests. Summers can be warm, while winters offer a quieter, often crisp hiking experience.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Gumiel De Izán?
A: The hiking routes in Gumiel De Izán are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 10 reviews. Hikers often praise the varied terrain, from river valleys and wetlands to pine and holm oak forests, as well as the integration of historical sites like old mills and Roman bridges along the paths.

Q: Are there any trails that lead through forests?
A: Absolutely. Beyond the agricultural plains, you'll find trails that wind through pine and holm oak forests. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1293683469/monte-de-bahabon-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monte de Bahabón Trail&lt;/a&gt;, for example, leads directly through these types of woodlands, offering views of the surrounding lomas.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter while hiking?
A: The Gromejón River valley and its banks provide a habitat for various wildlife. You might spot birds such as storks along the river, and the forested and agricultural areas support other local fauna. Keep an eye out for diverse birdlife and small mammals typical of the Castilian landscape.

Q: Are there any notable attractions or towns nearby that I can visit after a hike?
A: Yes, there are several interesting places to explore. You could visit the historic town of Aranda de Duero, which features the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4915397" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Santa María la Real&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5507816" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aranda Bridge&lt;/a&gt;, and even &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7076914" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Street Art Murals&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5507826" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Medieval Bridge of the Tanneries&lt;/a&gt; is another historical highlight in the vicinity.

Q: How many hiking routes are available in the Gumiel De Izán area?
A: There are over 20 hiking routes available around Gumiel De Izán, offering a wide range of options for different preferences and fitness levels. These include 7 easy routes and 17 moderate trails.
tips: The most important feature of Gumiel de Izán is its spectacular church. They are surprised when in a small town you find a church much larger than it should be, as is the case with this one, the Iglesia de Santa María.
This is a small town that is close to the city of Aranda de Duero on its northern side. The Hermitage of Nuestra Señora de los Olomos appears there.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the hike?
faqs.answer: The circular route starts and ends in Gumiel de Izán. You should be able to find parking within the town, which is well-equipped for visitors given its status as a Historic-Artistic Site.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, this trail is generally dog-friendly. However, as it passes through towns like Gumiel de Izán and Quintana del Pidio, and alongside vineyards and agricultural areas, it's advisable to keep your dog on a leash to respect local regulations and private property.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The Ribera del Duero region experiences varied seasons. Spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) are generally ideal, offering pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery, especially during harvest season in the vineyards. Summer can be hot, while winter might bring colder temperatures, though the paths are mostly accessible.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no permits or entrance fees required to hike this circular route. It is a publicly accessible trail through the countryside and towns.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This trail is rated as moderate. While most paths are accessible, it covers approximately 16.6 kilometers with around 100 meters of elevation gain and loss. Hikers should have a good level of physical fitness and sure-footedness. It might be challenging for absolute beginners but is manageable for those with some hiking experience.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the trail?
faqs.answer: The terrain is diverse, characteristic of the Ribera del Duero wine region. You'll encounter mostly accessible paths, including stretches alongside vineyards and almond groves, as well as pine forests and areas historically cultivated for cereals. Expect varied surfaces, but generally firm footing.

faqs.question: What cultural and natural highlights can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: This route is rich in highlights! You'll start in Gumiel de Izán, a Historic-Artistic Site known for its medieval layout, traditional Castilian architecture, and the magnificent Santa María Church with its Baroque facade. In Quintana del Pidio, you can explore traditional underground wineries and the Church of Santiago Apóstol. Naturally, you'll enjoy panoramic views of vineyards, almond groves, pine forests, and the Gromejón River valley with its riparian vegetation.

faqs.question: Is it a loop trail or out-and-back?
faqs.answer: This is a circular route, meaning it starts and ends in the same location, Gumiel de Izán, without retracing your steps.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several other paths, including the Ruta de Los Molinos, Camino de Quintana del Pidio al Gumiel de Izán, Camino de Fuentenavares, and Camino de Gramal, among others.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, both Gumiel de Izán and Quintana del Pidio are towns with a strong wine-making tradition and local amenities. You can find cafes, restaurants, and traditional establishments in these villages to refuel during or after your hike.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this hike?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and length, bring plenty of water, snacks, and wear appropriate hiking shoes suitable for varied terrain. Sun protection (hat, sunscreen) is recommended, especially during warmer months, as parts of the trail are exposed. A camera would be great for capturing the scenic views and historic architecture.
